definition: To *invoke* is to call upon a higher power — a god, angel, spirit, or saint — by name, asking it to attend, aid, or descend into the operator. It is the exact counterpart of *evoke*: invocation calls a power *in* (into the self, a statue, or a name), evocation calls a spirit *out* into external appearance. The magus invokes the divine but evokes the demon into the circle.
history: Latin *invocare* joined *in-* to *vocare*, 'to call,' meaning 'to call upon.' The pivotal refinement is ceremonial: practitioners fixed *invocare* against *evocare* as opposite directions of address — to call a power into oneself versus to call a spirit out — a distinction the theurgic tradition, addressing angels and gods, kept precise. The Greek Magical Papyri open rites with 'invoke in this way,' and *invocatio* stands among the most frequent operations in the Renaissance corpus. Magic: The Gathering uses it loosely for calling a power to one's side.

def_sources.label: Fanger, Invoking Angels (spirits invoked and addressed)
def_sources.abstract: A collection of essays about medieval and early Renaissance rituals for contacting or working with angels (scholars call this "theurgy," meaning rituals aimed at divine or heavenly beings rather than ordinary spirits). The essays show how these angel-summoning traditions mixed Christian, Jewish, and Islamic ideas together.

commentary: The tidy pairing of *invoke* (calling a power in, upward, devoutly) against *evoke* (calling a spirit out, to compel it) is real in the grimoires — Solomonic handbooks speak of aerial spirits 'evoked and constrained' — but scholars caution against projecting that ceremonial precision back onto messier sources. Claire Fanger's work on the *Ars Notoria* and John of Morigny reframes much angelic invocation as *theurgy*, devout petition, dissolving the confident theurgy/goetia boundary the later systematizers drew. The distinction is a retrospective ordering of practices whose own authors often could not tell conjuration from prayer.

commentary_sources.label: Claire Fanger, Invoking Angels
commentary_sources.abstract: A collection of essays about medieval and early Renaissance rituals for contacting or working with angels (scholars call this "theurgy," meaning rituals aimed at divine or heavenly beings rather than ordinary spirits). The essays show how these angel-summoning traditions mixed Christian, Jewish, and Islamic ideas together.

commentary_sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
commentary_sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

etymology.origin: Latin invocare 'to call upon' (in- + vocare 'to call')
etymology.note: The exact opposite of evoke: to invoke is to call a power INTO oneself, to evoke is to call it OUT. Ceremonial magic keeps the distinction precise.
etymology.hook: Invoke vs evoke: calling in against calling out.

flavor_snippets.html: The hymn's melody has persisted since Tourach's time, but the words changed to <mark>invoke</mark> the phobias of each listener.
<mark>Invoking</mark> Thor's wrath is a mistake few villains are stupid enough—or sturdy enough—to make twice.
"When properly <mark>invoked</mark>, the plasma globule encloses the offensive spell with a satisfying slurp."

mechanics_snippets.html:
<mark>Invoke</mark> Duplicity — Equipped creature gets +1/+1 and has "Whenever this creature deals combat damage to a player, you may sacrifice Trickster's Talisman. If you do, create a token that's a copy of this creature."
Equip {2}

Choose one —
• Draw X cards.
• <mark>Invoke</mark> the Firemind deals X damage to any target.

You may cast up to two instant and/or sorcery spells with total mana value 6 or less from your graveyard and/or hand without paying their mana costs. If those spells would be put into your graveyard, exile them instead. Exile <mark>Invoke</mark> Calamity.
